title: Airfield Status Board
summary: The default landing page assembles realtime runway status, ARFF CAT, weather advisories, NAVAIDs, RSC/BWC, and live contractor tracking into a single operational picture.
concept:
The Airfield Status Board at app/(app)/page.tsx is the operational nerve center of Glidepath. It is the first page an Airfield Manager sees on login and the page most likely to be kept open throughout a shift. Every datum on the board is either sourced from the airfield_status Supabase row (managed by DashboardContext) or fetched and subscribed to independently by the board itself.

Board Sections

  • Weather Strip — Fetches live METAR-derived conditions via fetchCurrentWeather() using the first runway's midpoint coordinates. Displays temperature, wind, visibility, and a Lucide icon chosen by weatherIcon(). The right cluster is the entry point for WWA Notifications (WATCH / WARNING / ADVISORY).
  • WWA Advisories — Severity-sorted banners (ADVISORY_SEVERITY: WARNING=0, WATCH=1, ADVISORY=2) so red never renders below yellow. Each has an optional expiry time; a 15-second interval (expiryTimerRef) checks for expired advisories and auto-removes them, logging to the activity log.
  • Runway Status Grid — Driven by runwayStatuses from DashboardContext. Each runway shows its active end, status (Open / Closed / Temporarily Closed / Suspended), and RSC + RCR surface condition values. Status changes call logRunwayStatusChange() from lib/supabase/airfield-status.ts.
  • ARFF Category — The ARFF CAT dropdown reads from arffCat / arffStatuses in context. Visibility is gated by arff_config.show_cat_dropdown on the base record — defaults to visible. Per-aircraft readiness dialogs call logArffStatusChange().
  • RSC / BWC — Runway Surface Condition and Braking/Weather Condition. Both require the narrower AIRFIELD_STATUS_WRITE_RSC_BWC_ONLY permission (or full AIRFIELD_STATUS_WRITE). Constants imported from lib/constants.ts: RSC_CONDITIONS, BWC_OPTIONS, RCR_CONDITION_TYPES.
  • NAVAIDs — Fetched from the navaid_statuses table via fetchNavaidStatuses() in lib/supabase/navaids.ts, then filtered to only show NAVAIDs present in base_navaids config so deleted entries do not linger. Three statuses: green (operational), yellow (degraded), red (outage). A Supabase Realtime subscription on navaid_statuses UPDATE events keeps all open browser tabs in sync.
  • AFM Out of Office / Closed Overlays — Two sticky banners with distinct severity tiers. Out of Office uses an accent-blue left rule and is minimizable; Airfield Management Closed uses a thick red left rule and has no minimize (a closed airfield is not background information). Both source from DashboardContext; deactivation calls logManualEntry().
  • Contractor Panel — fetchActiveContractors() loads personnel currently on the airfield. A Realtime channel on airfield_contractors fires INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE callbacks to keep the panel live without polling.
  • PPR Panel — Today's active PPR entries (filtered by isActivePpr(status), i.e., not Denied or Cancelled). “Today” is computed in base-local time using the installation's timezone to match how operators read arrival_date.

Realtime Architecture (board-specific)

The board registers three Supabase Realtime channels. dashboard_status:{installationId} listens for INSERT on airfield_checks and inspections tables, calling refreshStatus() from DashboardContext to pull fresh AMSL/check state. navaid_statuses:{installationId} listens for UPDATE events to reload the NAVAID grid. airfield_contractors:{installationId} listens for INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE to keep the contractor panel live. All three use subscribeWithErrorHandling() from lib/realtime-subscribe.ts for automatic reconnect with back-off. A visibilitychange listener also triggers reloads when the tab regains focus.

Permission Model

Permission KeyGrants
AIRFIELD_STATUS_WRITEFull board: runways, ARFF, advisories, NAVAIDs, remarks, OoO/Closed
AIRFIELD_STATUS_WRITE_RSC_BWC_ONLYRSC + BWC fields only (safety role)

code.from: // Realtime: subscribe to airfield_checks and inspections INSERT events

code.caption: NAVAID load — filters to configured base_navaids, cross-device realtime sync
code.note: After fetch, resolves against fetchInstallationNavaids() to drop orphaned entries.

annotations.note: Deleted NAVAIDs in base_navaids config are filtered out here so they don't linger on the board.

code.caption: AFM Closed sticky banner — danger-tier severity, no minimize affordance
NAVAID CRUD — fetchNavaidStatuses and updateNavaidStatus
code.note: Updating to green always nulls out notes; non-green updates carry the notes string.

fits: The Airfield Status Board is the consumer of DashboardContext (which owns runway/ARFF/advisory/OoO/Closed state and the refreshStatus function) and independently owns NAVAID, contractor, and PPR state. The board re-initializes on installation switch because all map/status components depend on [token, installationId].

quiz.q: The board has three separate Supabase Realtime channels. Which table does the `dashboard_status` channel listen to, and what does it do on each event?
choices: airfield_status UPDATE — calls refreshStatus()
airfield_checks INSERT and inspections INSERT — calls refreshStatus()
navaid_statuses UPDATE — calls loadNavaids()
airfield_contractors INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE — calls loadContractors()
quiz.explain: The `dashboard_status:{installationId}` channel listens for INSERT events on both `airfield_checks` and `inspections`, and on each event calls `refreshStatus()` from DashboardContext to pull fresh check/AMSL state. NAVAIDs and contractors each have their own separate channels.

quiz.q: A user with the safety role holds `airfield_status:write:rsc_bwc_only`. What fields can they edit on the status board?
choices: All fields — RSC, BWC, runway status, ARFF CAT, advisories, and NAVAIDs
RSC and BWC only
Runway status and ARFF CAT only
NAVAIDs and construction remarks only
quiz.explain: `canEditRscBwc` is true when the user holds either AIRFIELD_STATUS_WRITE or AIRFIELD_STATUS_WRITE_RSC_BWC_ONLY. Full AIRFIELD_STATUS_WRITE is required for runway/ARFF/advisory/OoO/Closed changes. The safety role gets the narrow key and can only edit RSC + BWC.

quiz.q: The PPR panel computes 'today' using `new Intl.DateTimeFormat('en-CA', { timeZone: baseTimezone }).format(new Date())`. Why `en-CA` specifically?
choices: It is the only locale that supports military time zones
en-CA formats dates as YYYY-MM-DD, which matches the storage shape of `arrival_date` directly
It is the default Next.js locale for Canada-hosted deployments
It forces the browser to use UTC regardless of user locale
quiz.explain: en-CA produces YYYY-MM-DD output, the same format used in the `arrival_date` column. Using the installation's IANA timezone ensures the panel shows today's flights in base local time, not UTC — an operator at 2300Z on a US base sees today's flights, not tomorrow's.

title: Visual NAVAIDs / Infrastructure
summary: The Visual NAVAIDs map page plots every airfield light and sign on Google Maps, computes DAFMAN 13-204 Vol 2 Table A3.1 outage compliance in real time, and auto-creates a discrepancy when an Airfield Manager reports a fixture outage.
concept:
The Visual NAVAIDs page at app/(app)/infrastructure/page.tsx (~4,000 lines) is the most feature-dense map in Glidepath. Its headline capability is auto-detecting when outages exceed A3.1 allowable thresholds — the system shows a 4-tier alert (green / yellow / red / black) for every configured lighting system and highlights individual components approaching or exceeding limits without any manual calculation by the Airfield Manager.

Feature Layer Architecture

All airfield fixtures are stored in infrastructure_features (fetched via fetchInfrastructureFeatures()) and rendered on a Google Maps instance as one of two primitives:

  • Meter-sized Circles — runway and taxiway lights rendered as google.maps.Circle objects with LIGHT_RADIUS_METERS = 1.5. They scale naturally with map zoom and stay fast with 1,300+ features.
  • Raster Icon Markers — signs, PAPIs, beacons, windcones. Rendered as canvas-drawn ImageData registered as named icons via registerIcon(). Labeled signs are individually pre-rendered via createLabeledSign() with the correct sign colors and any rotation baked in.

Layer visibility is toggled by visibleLayers / visibleSourceLayers state. The showOutagesOnly toggle hides all operational features so operators can quickly review what is inoperative.

Outage Compliance Engine

The engine lives entirely in lib/outage-rules.ts and implements DAFMAN 13-204 Vol 2 Table A3.1. Key concepts:

  • SystemHealth — computed by calculateSystemHealth(); aggregates all components in a lighting system into a single health record with exceededComponents and approachingComponents arrays.
  • OutageStatus — per-component; includes actual vs. allowable counts/percentages, adjacent-light violations, consecutive-light violations, bar-level outage counts, and the 5 required actions (NOTAM, notify CE, system shutoff, notify TERPs, obstruction NOTAM attributes).
  • 4-Tier AlertTier: green (operational), yellow (approaching limit — within 5% of threshold), red (exceeded), black (system inoperative — overall component exceeded).
  • Bar-level analysis — analyzeBarOutages() groups features by bar_group_id. A bar is considered inoperative when 3 or more of its lamps are inoperative (BAR_INOP_THRESHOLD = 3). Bar-out counts are then used for the allowable_outage_count threshold check, while the percentage threshold continues to use individual lamp counts.
  • Adjacent / consecutive detection — detectAdjacentViolation() and detectConsecutiveViolation() sort features along their dominant geographic axis (lat or lon spread) and scan for adjacent pairs or runs exceeding the component's allowable_outage_consecutive limit.

Report Outage → Auto-Discrepancy Flow

When an Airfield Manager clicks Report Outage on a map feature popup, the reportOutageRef handler:

  1. Calls updateFeatureStatus(id, 'inoperative').
  2. Checks if the feature belongs to a bar group — if 3+ lights in the bar are now inop, appends a DAFMAN note to the discrepancy description.
  3. Calls createDiscrepancy() with a rich title, description, location, and infrastructure_feature_id linking the two records.
  4. Calls createOutageEvent() to stamp the timeline.
  5. Recalculates the component outage via calculateComponentOutage() on the refreshed feature set and shows an outageAlert dialog if any threshold is now exceeded or approaching.

Marking a feature operational (markOperationalRef) mirrors this: it creates a 'resolved' outage event and prompts the Airfield Manager to close any linked open discrepancies.

System Health Panel

components/infrastructure/system-health-panel.tsx groups systems into five categories (Runway, Taxiways, Approach, Signage, Other) and renders a card per category showing inoperative feature count and worst alert tier. Individual system rows are expandable to show component-level detail including allowable outage text and DAFMAN notes 1–5.

code.caption: 4-tier alert mapping and bar-level outage analysis (BAR_INOP_THRESHOLD=3)

annotations.note: getAlertTier maps SystemHealth status to 4-tier: inoperative→black, exceeded→red, approaching→yellow, else green.
BAR_INOP_THRESHOLD=3: a 5-lamp bar is considered inoperative once 3 lamps are out, per DAFMAN 13-204v2.
analyzeBarOutages groups features by bar_group_id, counts how many bars have 3+ inop lights, and builds synthetic bar-unit features for spatial ordering.

code.caption: calculateComponentOutage — the core per-component threshold evaluation

annotations.note: Percentage threshold always uses individual lamp counts; count threshold uses bars-out count if bar groups exist.
isExceeded fires on zero-tolerance, pct breach, count breach, adjacent violation, or consecutive violation. isApproaching fires when within 5 percentage points of the threshold.

code.caption: Report Outage handler — marks inop, bar-out check, auto-creates discrepancy, recalculates thresholds
code.from: // Report Outage handler — marks feature inoperative + auto-creates discrepancy

code.caption: Feature and system health load — calculateAllSystemHealth called on dbFeatures change
code.from: // Load lighting systems + components and compute health

fits: The infrastructure page imports calculateAllSystemHealth from lib/outage-rules.ts and passes it systems + components + dbFeatures on every feature load. The SystemHealthPanel component receives the resulting SystemHealth[] array. Feature status changes (report outage / mark operational) trigger a fresh fetchInfrastructureFeatures() → recalculate cycle. The discrepancy created by Report Outage is linked via infrastructure_feature_id and shown in the Discrepancies module.

quiz.q: According to the outage engine, when is a 5-lamp approach-light bar considered 'inoperative' for the purposes of the allowable_outage_count threshold?
choices: When any 1 lamp is inoperative
When 3 or more lamps in the bar group are inoperative
When all 5 lamps are inoperative
When the overall system outage percentage exceeds 10%
quiz.explain: BAR_INOP_THRESHOLD = 3 in lib/outage-rules.ts. analyzeBarOutages() counts how many bars have 3+ inop lights (barsOut), and that number is used for the allowable_outage_count check. The percentage threshold continues to use individual lamp counts.

quiz.q: What are the two spatial violation types that calculateComponentOutage can detect beyond simple count/percentage thresholds?
choices: Geographic clustering and zone overlap
Adjacent-light violation and consecutive-light violation
Bar-group saturation and perimeter breach
Threshold proximity and approach-path alignment
quiz.explain: detectAdjacentViolation() fires when any two directly neighboring features (sorted along the dominant axis) are both inoperative. detectConsecutiveViolation() fires when a run of inoperative features exceeds component.allowable_outage_consecutive. Both use bar units when bar_group_id is set.

quiz.q: When an Airfield Manager clicks Report Outage on a map popup, what happens in addition to marking the feature inoperative?
choices: Only the feature status is updated; a discrepancy must be created manually
A discrepancy is auto-created with the feature linked via infrastructure_feature_id, an outage event is stamped, and a threshold alert dialog appears if any component is now exceeded or approaching
The system sends a NOTAM draft to the NOTAMs module automatically
The feature is marked red on the Airfield Status board NAVAID grid
quiz.explain: reportOutageRef calls updateFeatureStatus, createDiscrepancy (with infrastructure_feature_id), createOutageEvent, then recalculates the component's OutageStatus. If isExceeded or isApproaching, setOutageAlert() shows the compliance dialog. The NAVAID grid on the status board is separate from the Visual NAVAIDs infrastructure page.

title: Parking Plans
summary: The Parking Plans module provides a live Google Maps canvas for placing aircraft, obstacles, and taxilanes, then evaluates every aircraft-to-aircraft and aircraft-to-obstacle clearance against UFC 3-260-01 Table 6-1a in real time.
concept:
The Parking Plans page at app/(app)/parking/page.tsx (~5,150 lines) is a full interactive GIS editor for aircraft apron planning. Plans, spots, obstacles, taxilanes, and apron boundaries are all persisted to Supabase — nothing is ephemeral. The five core tables are parking_plans, parking_spots, parking_obstacles, parking_taxilanes, and parking_apron_boundaries, all CRUD-managed in lib/supabase/parking.ts.

Aircraft Rendering

Each parking spot is matched to aircraft dimensions from the allAircraft database (200+ types). The dimensions produce a SpotWithAircraft object containing wingspan_ft, length_ft, and pivot_point_ft (nose-gear distance). Silhouette SVGs from public/aircraft_silhouettes/ are fetched, tighten-viewbox-cropped, rendered to a fixed-size canvas at the correct wingspan-to-length aspect ratio, then re-rotated synchronously on heading change via imperativeRotateSpot() — bypassing React re-renders for smooth slider drag. Icon scale is derived from the Web Mercator formula (computeIconScale()) using cos(lat) / 2^zoom so aircraft silhouettes render at true real-world wingspan regardless of map zoom or rotation.

Clearance Engine — UFC 3-260-01 Table 6-1a

The clearance engine lives in lib/calculations/parking-clearance.ts and implements every applicable item from Table 6-1a of UFC 3-260-01. Key functions:

  • getWingtipClearanceDetail(wingspan, context, aircraftName) — returns a ClearanceDetail with clearance_ft, ufc_item, and description. Contexts: parking (Item 4P, 10ft/20ft), parking_transient (25ft C-5/C-17), parking_kc_refuel (25ft KC-10/KC-46/KC-135), interior_taxilane (Item 5I, 20ft), peripheral_taxilane (Item 6T, 30ft/50ft), peripheral_transient (25ft).
  • checkWingtipClearance(spotA, spotB) — samples perimeter points along both aircraft bounding rectangles and finds the minimum distance between them. Uses the more restrictive clearance from the two aircraft. Returns ok, warning (within 10% of required), or violation.
  • checkObstacleClearance(spot, obstacle) — handles four obstacle shapes: point, building (rotatable rectangle), circle (radius-aware), and line (polyline).
  • checkTaxilaneClearance(spot, taxilane) — computes getTaxilaneEnvelopeHalfWidth() as 0.5 × designWingspan + clearance, then checks whether any extremity of the parked aircraft breaches that envelope.
  • findAllViolations(spots, obstacles, taxilanes) — runs all pairwise spot-to-spot, spot-to-obstacle, and spot-to-taxilane checks, returns only violations and warnings sorted by severity then distance.

ADG Classification

Aircraft are bucketed into ADG (Aircraft Design Group) I–VI by wingspan: I (<49ft), II (<79ft), III (<118ft), IV (<171ft), V (<214ft), VI (<262ft) per Table 3-1. The ADG determines which clearance tier applies when a custom wingspan override is not set on the spot.

Nose-Gear Offset

getAircraftCenter() computes the true aircraft center from the stored nose-gear block position: offset = length/2 − pivotPointFt applied backward from heading. All clearance geometry uses the true center so aircraft aren't mispositioned by the length of the nose boom.

PDF Export Pipeline

generateParkingPdf() in lib/parking-pdf.ts supports both single-apron and multi-apron exports. The operator places the 1600×900 capture frame over each apron section, clicks Add Apron, repositions, and clicks Confirm. Each apron is captured via a position: fixed resize trick that temporarily expands the map to the capture dimensions, waits for tile repaint, then uses html2canvas to rasterize. A warm-up cycle on map mount primes Google's WebGL tile cache so the first capture produces real tiles, not gray.

Plan Persistence Model

TableScoped ByNotes
parking_plansbase_idOne active plan at a time; templates can be duplicated
parking_spotsplan_id + base_idAircraft position, heading, tail number, unit callsign, status
parking_obstaclesbase_idShared across plans — building/point/circle/line shapes
parking_taxilanesplan_id + base_idInterior vs. peripheral; design_wingspan_ft drives envelope
parking_apron_boundariesplan_id + base_idPolygon coords for Item 7 boundary-distance checks

code.caption: UFC 3-260-01 Table 6-1a wingtip clearance lookup — all context variants
code.from: /** Get wingtip clearance with UFC reference per Table 6-1a */

annotations.note: large = wingspan >= 110ft (33.5m). Drives the standard parking 10ft/20ft split.
KC refuel override applies regardless of context when aircraft name starts with KC-10/KC-46/KC-135.

code.caption: checkWingtipClearance — perimeter sampling approach for aircraft-to-aircraft minimum distance
code.to:
return {
    distance_ft: Math.round(minDistance * 10) / 10,
    required_ft: governing.clearance_ft,
    status,
    aircraft_a: spotA.aircraft_name || 'Aircraft',
    aircraft_b: spotB.aircraft_name || 'Aircraft',
    spot_a_id: spotA.id,
    spot_b_id: spotB.id,
    ufc_item: governing.ufc_item,
    ufc_desc: governing.description,
  }
}

annotations.note: governing = the more restrictive of the two aircraft clearance requirements.
warning fires at 110% of required distance — gives the operator an early visual signal before a true violation.

code.caption: getTaxilaneEnvelopeHalfWidth — Item 5/6 envelope from taxilane type and design wingspan
code.from: /** Compute the required taxilane envelope half-width */

code.caption: spotsWithAircraft derivation — maps DB spots to SpotWithAircraft with real aircraft dimensions

fits: Clearance results are computed in a useMemo on spotsWithAircraft, obstacles, apronContext, and taxilanesForCheck. Violations and warnings are derived memos that filter the full results array. The PDF generator receives { spots, spotsWithAircraft, allResults, violations, warnings, mapDataUrl } per section. Plan operations (create, update, duplicate, set active) all route through lib/supabase/parking.ts which calls logActivity() for audit trail entries.

quiz.q: A C-17A (wingspan 170ft) is parked on a standard apron. An F-15C (wingspan 43ft) is placed adjacent. Which aircraft's clearance requirement governs the wingtip-to-wingtip check, and what is the required distance?
choices: F-15C governs at 10ft — small aircraft rule
C-17A governs at 20ft — larger wingspan exceeds 110ft threshold
Both apply equally at 15ft average
C-17A governs at 25ft — transient apron C-17 override
quiz.explain: checkWingtipClearance picks the more restrictive (governing) clearance between the two aircraft. C-17A at 170ft wingspan is ≥ 110ft so its detail returns 20ft (Item 4P, WS ≥ 110ft). F-15C at 43ft returns 10ft. 20ft > 10ft, so C-17A's requirement governs. The 25ft transient override applies only when context is parking_transient.

quiz.q: What does `getAircraftCenter()` correct for, and why does it matter for clearance geometry?
choices: Map projection distortion at high latitudes
The nose-gear offset from the aircraft center — so the clearance rectangle is anchored to the true center, not the nose gear block position stored in the DB
The difference between wingspan and overall length for non-rectangular aircraft
The Mercator pixels-per-meter scaling for icon size
quiz.explain: Parking spots store the nose-gear block position (longitude/latitude). getAircraftCenter() offsets backward from heading by (length/2 - pivotPointFt) to find the true aircraft center. All clearance bounding-box math (corners, wingtips, perimeter points) uses spotCenter() which calls this function, so a C-5's clearance envelope is not shifted forward by 30+ feet.

quiz.q: Which of the five parking DB tables is scoped to base_id rather than plan_id, meaning its data is shared across all plans for a base?
choices: parking_plans
parking_taxilanes
parking_apron_boundaries
parking_obstacles
quiz.explain: parking_obstacles is scoped only to base_id. Buildings, poles, and other fixed obstacles are physical features of the apron that persist across all parking plans. Taxilanes and apron boundaries are plan-scoped because different plans may have different taxilane layouts.
